topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating, high-pitched whine, mirroring the tension tightening in Sarah’s chest. She stared at the framed memo, the congratulatory note for David’s impending promotion, and felt a slow, hot flush creep up her neck. *He always gets the spotlight,* she thought, gripping her lukewarm coffee mug until her knuckles were white. When Mark announced the decision, his smile was too bright, his tone too easy. Sarah stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor, the sound echoing in the sudden silence. "Well," she managed, her voice surprisingly steady, though a tremor ran through it, "that certainly clarifies things." She turned and walked towards the exit, the heavy click of the door closing behind her sounding like a definitive finality.

[story 2]

Liam paced the length of his cubicle, his foot tapping a frantic rhythm against the cheap carpet. Every glance toward the executive suite felt like a physical assault. He re-read the job description, the same one that had been floating around for weeks, and a bitter taste coated his tongue. He clenched his jaw so tightly he could feel the muscles in his temples bunching. When the announcement came, Liam didn't move immediately. He simply stood, his gaze fixed on the polished mahogany of his desk, his breathing shallow and rapid. "Of course," he murmured, the words tasting like ash. He picked up a stray paperclip, twisting it violently between his fingers until the metal bent slightly, a small, sharp release of the internal pressure building behind his eyes.

[story 3]

Maria watched the celebratory champagne cork pop, the fizz seeming to mock her stagnant position. A sharp, almost painful tightness constricted her throat. She felt an overwhelming urge to shatter something nearby, anything to break the suffocating symmetry of the situation. When the manager finally addressed the room, Maria offered a tight, brittle smile that didn't reach her eyes. "Congratulations to both," she said, her voice pitched just a little too high, brittle as thin ice. She didn't wait for further praise; instead, she gathered her belongings with swift, jerky movements, avoiding eye contact entirely, her posture rigid as she made her escape from the room.
